description:
# Fix: WorktreeGuard Commit Fails with HUSKY/lint-staged â€” Normalize HUSKY=0 Pattern

## Situation

The Automaker server manages agent worktrees via a set of services that must commit code on behalf of AI agents. These commits happen in git worktrees under `.worktrees/`, which are excluded from `.prettierignore` and `.gitignore`. Five files currently issue `git commit` calls with varying approaches to bypassing Husky hooks:

| File | HUSKY=0 in shared execEnv? | HUSKY=0 at call site? |
|---|---|---|
| `apps/server/src/services/git-workflow-service.ts` | âś… Yes (line 97â€“101) | â€” |
| `apps/server/src/lib/worktree-guard.ts` | âś… Yes (`guardEnv`, line 62) | â€” |
| `apps/server/src/routes/worktree/create-pr.ts` | âś… Yes (via `common.ts`) | â€” |
| `apps/server/src/routes/worktree/common.ts` | âś… Yes (exported `execEnv`, line 105) | â€” |
| `apps/server/src/services/worktree-recovery-service.ts` | âťŚ **No** (line 42) | âś… Yes (line 138 â€” compensating spread) |

Three consecutive features (`k5yo1ccaq`, `dwtmmw3za`, `sl35xd5t4`) all failed to auto-commit because Husky/lint-staged intercepted the `git commit --no-verify` call in worktrees, causing lint-staged to revert all staged changes and produce an empty commit. This required manual rescue via `HUSKY=0 git -C <worktree> commit -m "message"`.

The official Husky bypass is the `HUSKY=0` environment variable â€” not `--no-verify`, which Husky ignores in some configurations.

## Problem

**Primary issue (now resolved in most files):** `git commit --no-verify` alone does not reliably bypass Husky. When Husky runs lint-staged, lint-staged tries to format staged files inside `.worktrees/`, which are excluded by `.prettierignore`. Prettier returns a non-zero exit code, lint-staged reverts all staged changes, and the commit produces nothing. Three features were blocked as a result and required manual operator rescue.

**Remaining inconsistency (one file):** `worktree-recovery-service.ts` defines its module-level `execEnv` **without** `HUSKY: '0'`:

```typescript
// apps/server/src/services/worktree-recovery-service.ts (line 42) â€” CURRENT
const execEnv = { ...process.env, PATH: extendedPath };
```

It compensates by explicitly spreading `HUSKY: '0'` directly on the one known commit call (line 138). This is functionally correct today, but:

1. **It is inconsistent** with every other file in the codebase, where `HUSKY: '0'` lives in the shared `execEnv` object.
2. **It is a latent footgun.** If a developer adds a new `git commit` call to `worktree-recovery-service.ts` in the future and follows the established pattern of passing `{ env: execEnv }`, they will silently miss `HUSKY=0` and reproduce the original bug.
3. **It violates the single-source-of-truth principle** for exec environment configuration in this codebase.

This matters because `WorktreeRecoveryService` is the **last line of defense** â€” it runs after every agent exit to rescue uncommitted work. A silent Husky bypass failure here means the feature gets marked `blocked` with no automated recovery path.

## Approach

### Change

Add `HUSKY: '0'` to the module-level `execEnv` constant in `worktree-recovery-service.ts` (line 42), making the existing call-site spread redundant (but harmless):

```typescript
// apps/server/src/services/worktree-recovery-service.ts (line 42) â€” AFTER
const execEnv = { ...process.env, PATH: extendedPath, HUSKY: '0' };
```

The explicit spread at the commit call site (line 138) may be left in place or removed â€” either is correct. Leaving it provides belt-and-suspenders safety; removing it reduces noise. **Preference: leave it in place** to document intent at the call site.

### Why this is the right pattern

All other files in the codebase follow the same pattern:
- Build a module-level `execEnv` that extends `process.env` with an extended `PATH` + all required overrides (`HUSKY: '0'`, etc.)
- Pass `{ env: execEnv }` to every `execAsync` / `execFileAsync` call
- No per-call env spreading required

Centralizing `HUSKY: '0'` in the shared env object means future developers cannot accidentally miss it.

### No other changes needed

- `git-workflow-service.ts` â€” already correct
- `worktree-guard.ts` â€” already correct
- `create-pr.ts` â€” already correct (inherits from `common.ts`)
- `common.ts` â€” already correct
- No new types, services, routes, or `EventType` registrations required
- No build order changes required (this is a server-only change)

## Results

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `apps/server/src/services/worktree-recovery-service.ts` line 42 includes `HUSKY: '0'` in the module-level `execEnv` object
- [ ] All five git-committing files follow the consistent pattern: `HUSKY: '0'` in module-level `execEnv`
- [ ] Server builds successfully after the change (`npm run build:server`)
- [ ] No TypeScript errors introduced
- [ ] Agent features can auto-commit from worktrees without manual rescue
- [ ] `WorktreeRecoveryService` recovery commits succeed end-to-end when Husky is active in the root repository

### Definition of Done

A code reviewer reading `worktree-recovery-service.ts` sees `HUSKY: '0'` in the module-level `execEnv` and has no doubt that all git operations in this file bypass Husky, consistent with the rest of the codebase.

## Constraints

### In Scope
- One-line edit to `apps/server/src/services/worktree-recovery-service.ts`
- Optionally retain or remove the redundant `HUSKY: '0'` spread at line 138 (non-breaking either way)

### Out of Scope
- Modifying `.prettierignore` or lint-staged config
- Changing how `--no-verify` is used (keep it; belt-and-suspenders is fine)
- Rewriting `execEnv` construction across other files (already correct)
- Adding test coverage for Husky bypass behavior (integration-only concern; not unit-testable without process-level env mocking)
- Any changes to the three failed features (`k5yo1ccaq`, `dwtmmw3za`, `sl35xd5t4`) â€” those were manually rescued

### Dependencies
- None. This is a standalone server change with no package dependencies.

### Risks
- **Risk:** Extremely low. This adds a key to an environment object that already has the key added at the call site. Net behavioral change is zero for existing code paths.
- **Risk of not fixing:** Medium-high. Any new git commit call added to `worktree-recovery-service.ts` in the future, following the standard pattern, silently loses the HUSKY bypass and reproduces the original three-feature failure scenario.

### Non-Goals
- Diagnosing why `--no-verify` is insufficient in some Husky configurations (known Husky behavior; `HUSKY=0` is the documented solution)
- Preventing all possible future Husky-related failures (this fix normalizes the established pattern; future deviations are a code review concern)
title: [linear] Fix: WorktreeGuard commit fails with HUSKY/lint-staged â€” use HUSKY=0 env var

descriptionHistory.description:
Fix: WorktreeGuard commit fails with HUSKY/lint-staged â€” use HUSKY=0 env var

## Problem

Three consecutive features (k5yo1ccaq, dwtmmw3za, sl35xd5t4) all hit the same git commit failure in worktrees. The agent completes its work but `git commit --no-verify` fails with "uncommitted work in worktree."

**Root cause:** The git workflow system calls `git commit --no-verify` but Husky ignores `--no-verify` in some configurations. lint-staged runs, tries to format staged files in `.worktrees/`, but `.prettierignore` excludes `.worktrees/` â€” lint-staged marks format check as failed, reverts all staged changes, and the commit produces nothing.

**Why** `HUSKY=0` works: `HUSKY=0` is the officially supported Husky bypass env var â€” disables Husky completely regardless of git flags.

## Files to fix

Search for `git commit` in the git workflow service â€” likely `apps/server/src/lib/git-workflow-service.ts` or similar. Change `git commit --no-verify` â†’ `HUSKY=0 git commit`.

## Workaround (current)

When an agent fails to commit: `HUSKY=0 git -C <worktree> commit -m "message"`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Find git workflow code that calls `git commit`
- [ ] Replace with `HUSKY=0 git commit`
- [ ] Agent features successfully auto-commit without manual rescue
